mnemonics: JPD = Just Picture Data (Visualize the joint table for two variables)
Sum Over All = 1.0 (The sum of all probabilities in a joint distribution must equal 1)
keyPoints: A joint probability distribution describes the probability of two events occurring together, denoted as P(X=x, Y=y).
Marginal probability is found by summing the joint probabilities over all values of the other variable.
Two random variables are independent if and only if their joint distribution equals the product of their marginal distributions for all values: P(X,Y) = P(X)P(Y).
commonMistakes: Assuming independence when it is not given, leading to incorrect use of P(X,Y) = P(X)P(Y).
Confusing joint probability P(A,B) with conditional probability P(A|B).
Forgetting that the sum of all probabilities in the entire joint distribution must equal 1.
